name: AppReleaseBasics
tagline: Take a notes app from 'runs on my emulator' toward 'shippable': runtime permissions plus release build config.
whatItDoes: You see a single screen titled 'My Notes - Reminders' that explains the app wants permission to post notification reminders. A status line shows 'Notifications: GRANTED' (green) or 'Notifications: DENIED' (red) reflecting the live permission state. A button reads 'Request notification permission' when denied; tapping it pops the real Android system permission dialog. Once you allow it, the status flips to GRANTED and the button becomes disabled, reading 'Permission already granted'. There is nothing to navigate to - the whole lesson is this one flow plus the build files behind it.
mentalModel: Shipping an app is two jobs the emulator hides from you: ask the user for dangerous permissions at run time (declare in the manifest AND request in code), and configure how the build is versioned, typed (debug vs release), and signed.

screens.name: NotificationPermissionScreen
screens.role: Stateful screen that reads the current POST_NOTIFICATIONS status, builds the permission launcher, and fires the system dialog on tap.

screens.name: NotificationPermissionContent
screens.role: Stateless UI that draws the title, explanation, GRANTED/DENIED status text, and the request Button from plain inputs.

screens.name: NotificationPermissionDeniedPreview / NotificationPermissionGrantedPreview
screens.role: Two @Preview functions that render the stateless content with hand-supplied isGranted values, no real Context.

concepts.plain: A dangerous permission needs BOTH a static <uses-permission> line in AndroidManifest.xml AND a run-time request in code. Declaring 'android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS' in the manifest is not enough on API 33+; the user still has to approve it via a dialog.

rememberLauncherForActivityResult with the ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() contract gives you a 'launcher' object. Calling launcher.launch(permission) shows the OS dialog, and the lambda receives a Boolean 'granted' when the user answers.

ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(context, permission) returns PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED or PERMISSION_DENIED. It is how the screen knows the real status on first launch (and after the user returns from settings) instead of guessing.

POST_NOTIFICATIONS only exists on Android 13 (API 33, Build.VERSION_CODES.TIRAMISU) and up. The code checks Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= TIRAMISU and treats older devices as already granted, so it never requests a permission the platform does not recognize.

versionCode is an integer (here 1) that Google Play uses ONLY to decide which build is newer - bump it every published release; users never see it. versionName is a human string (here '1.0') shown in the store and Settings; it is not used for ordering.

buildTypes { debug { ... } release { ... } } define two flavors. debug is auto-signed with a throwaway key, debuggable, no shrinking - never shippable. release is what you ship: it would be signed with your keystore and usually minified with R8 (isMinifyEnabled / isShrinkResources).

Every APK/AAB must be cryptographically signed. The signingConfigs { create('release') { ... } } block is left COMMENTED so assembleDebug works with no keystore; it shows where a release.jks store file, alias, and passwords (pulled from env vars, never hardcoded) would be wired in.

NotificationPermissionScreen owns the Context, state, and launcher; NotificationPermissionContent takes only isGranted plus an onRequestPermission callback. That separation is what lets the @Preview functions render the UI without building a real launcher or Context.

files.role: The whole app: the Activity, the stateful permission screen with its launcher, the stateless previewable UI, and two design-time previews.
Declares the POST_NOTIFICATIONS permission (step 1 of the model) and the MAIN/LAUNCHER intent-filter that makes MainActivity the app's entry point.
The release-engineering lesson: versionCode/versionName, debug and release build types, and a commented signingConfigs example.
Narrated tour of runtime permissions, versioning, build types, keystore/signing, and bundleRelease - all learnable with no Play Console account.

highlights.title: Declaring the dangerous permission (step 1)
highlights.explain: The <uses-permission android:name='android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS' /> line is the static half of the two-step model. It tells Android the app MAY post notifications, but on API 33+ this alone grants nothing - the code in MainActivity must still request it. On API 32 and below the line is harmless and notifications work without a prompt.

highlights.title: The MAIN/LAUNCHER intent-filter
highlights.explain: This <activity> block makes MainActivity the app's entry point. android:exported='true' is required because the launcher (outside the app) starts it, and the intent-filter pairing action MAIN with category LAUNCHER is what puts the icon in the app drawer. Remove the filter and the app vanishes from the launcher.

highlights.title: Reading the current grant status with an SDK guard
highlights.explain: isGranted is initialized once via remember { mutableStateOf(...) }. On API 33+ (TIRAMISU) it asks the real system with ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission == PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED; on older devices the permission does not exist, so it defaults to true. This is why the status text is correct on the very first frame.

highlights.title: Building the permission launcher
highlights.explain: rememberLauncherForActivityResult with ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() registers with the Activity Result framework and returns a launcher. Its lambda receives a Boolean 'granted' when the user answers the system dialog, and the single line isGranted = granted updates state so the UI recomposes. This is the modern, library-free permission API.

highlights.title: Firing the system dialog on tap
highlights.explain: The stateful screen hands the stateless UI its onRequestPermission callback. Inside, the same Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= TIRAMISU guard gates launcher.launch(permission) - the one line that actually pops the OS permission dialog. On older devices the tap is a no-op because the permission is meaningless there.

highlights.title: Versioning a release
highlights.explain: versionCode = 1 is the integer Google Play uses ONLY to rank builds - bump it on every upload or Play will reject the artifact as not newer. versionName = '1.0' is the string users see and is never used for ordering. testInstrumentationRunner wires up Espresso/JUnit instrumentation. Confusing these two is the classic beginner publishing mistake.

highlights.title: Debug vs release build types
highlights.explain: buildTypes defines the two flavors. debug sets isMinifyEnabled = false for fast, debuggable, auto-signed builds you never ship. release keeps minify OFF here for simplicity but the comments show the real-world toggles (isMinifyEnabled / isShrinkResources for R8) and the commented signingConfig = signingConfigs.getByName('release') line where your keystore would attach.

newVsPrev: RoomAndPreferences taught local persistence (a Room database plus DataStore/SharedPreferences). AppReleaseBasics deliberately drops the data layer and the Navigation 3 + kotlinx-serialization plugins to focus on what comes AFTER your app works: requesting a real dangerous runtime permission (POST_NOTIFICATIONS via the Jetpack ActivityResult API) and the Gradle release plumbing - versionCode/versionName, debug vs release build types, and a commented signingConfigs/keystore recipe - that turn a running app into a shippable one.
tryThis: On an Android 13+ emulator, deny the permission, then go to Settings > Apps > App Release Basics > Permissions and toggle Notifications. Cold-launch the app again and watch checkSelfPermission report GRANTED on the first frame without you tapping anything.

Change versionName to '1.1' and versionCode to 2 in build.gradle.kts, run ./gradlew assembleDebug, and confirm it builds. Then try uploading two builds with the SAME versionCode to see why Play would reject the second.

In the release { } block set isMinifyEnabled = true and add isShrinkResources = true, then run ./gradlew assembleRelease and read any R8 output - this shows what real shrinking does (note: it will be unsigned without the keystore).

Temporarily delete the <uses-permission> line from AndroidManifest.xml, rebuild, and observe that launcher.launch never actually grants the permission - declaration is genuinely required, not optional.

Add a third @Preview that calls NotificationPermissionContent with isGranted = false but a real-looking lambda, then try (and fail) to call NotificationPermissionScreen from a preview to feel why the stateful/stateless split exists.
gotchas: The signingConfigs block in build.gradle.kts is intentionally COMMENTED OUT so assembleDebug needs no keystore. This sample never actually signs a release or runs bundleRelease - those steps are described as concepts in the README, not wired into the build.

isMinifyEnabled = false is set in BOTH debug and release here on purpose to keep the teaching build simple. A real release would turn on R8 (isMinifyEnabled / isShrinkResources), which means a production app behaves differently from what you test in this sample.

POST_NOTIFICATIONS only exists on API 33+ (TIRAMISU). On API 24-32 emulators the request button is effectively a no-op and the status shows GRANTED immediately, because the code treats those devices as already granted - so test the real dialog on an Android 13+ image.

This project is named like a navigation sample but does NOT use Navigation 3 - the Nav and kotlinx-serialization dependencies from the base project were removed on purpose because the lesson is releasing, not navigating.
runTest: Build and install on an Android 13+ (API 33+) device or emulator: ./gradlew installDebug (or ./gradlew assembleDebug for just the APK, and ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest for the unit test). Launch the app, confirm the status reads 'Notifications: DENIED' in red, tap 'Request notification permission', and watch the real OS dialog appear; allow it and the status should flip to 'Notifications: GRANTED' in green with the button disabled. There are no deep links in this project. To exercise the release path conceptually, configure a keystore and run ./gradlew bundleRelease to produce an .aab under build/outputs/bundle/release/.
